pages.title: Welcome to Multi-Factor Authentication
pages.subtitle: Learn how multi-factor authentication helps protect accounts even if a password is stolen.

pages.title: What is multi-factor authentication?
pages.text:
Multi-factor authentication, or MFA, is a security measure that requires more than one form of verification before allowing access to an account.

Instead of relying only on a password, MFA adds another layer of protection.

This helps reduce the risk of unauthorized access if a password is stolen, guessed, or reused.

pages.title: Why MFA matters...
pages.text:
Passwords alone are not always enough to protect an account.

They may be exposed through phishing, password reuse, data breaches, or social engineering.

MFA adds protection beyond passwords.

A strong password is still important, but MFA provides a second layer of defense. If an attacker steals a password, MFA can help stop them from accessing the account unless they also have the second factor.

MFA helps stop attackers from logging in even if they know your password.

pages.title: Common MFA methods
pages.text:
Common forms of MFA include:

  • Authenticator apps that generate temporary login codes.
  • Push notifications asking you to approve a login.
  • Hardware security keys that plug into a computer or phone.
  • Text message (SMS) codes sent to a phone.

Examples of commonly used authenticator apps include:

  • Microsoft Authenticator.
  • Google Authenticator.
  • Duo Mobile.
  • Okta Verify.

These apps generate short codes that change every 30 seconds or so and are used along with your password during login.

Visit the Everyday Tools Playbook in the Training category for more information.

pages.title: Stronger MFA methods
pages.text:
Some MFA methods provide stronger protection than others.

  • Authenticator apps are generally safer than text messages because they do not rely on phone networks.
  • Hardware security keys provide one of the strongest forms of MFA protection.

Text message codes are still helpful, but they can sometimes be intercepted through phone number attacks or SIM swapping.

Whenever possible, organizations should prefer authenticator apps or hardware security keys.

pages.title: Unexpected MFA prompts
pages.text:
If you receive an MFA prompt that you did not expect, it may be a warning sign.

An attacker could be trying to log in with your password and hoping you approve the request by mistake.

Do not approve unexpected login requests.

Unexpected MFA prompts should be treated seriously and reported according to your organization's process.

Next slide shows the common ways attackers try to get around MFA.

pages.title: How attackers try to get around MFA
pages.text:
MFA adds important protection, but attackers may still try to bypass it.

  1. Phishing – Attackers may send users to fake login pages that look real in order to steal both the password and the MFA code.
  2. Push fatigue – Attackers may repeatedly send approval requests, hoping the user accepts one by mistake or just to make the prompts stop.
  3. SIM swapping – If MFA uses text messages, an attacker may try to take over the victim's phone number and receive the codes instead.
  4. Session theft – Sometimes attackers steal a valid logged-in session after MFA has already been completed, which can let them access the account without entering a new code.
  5. Social engineering – Attackers may pretend to be IT or support staff and ask the user to share a code, approve a login, or help with account access.

Never approve unexpected login requests or share MFA codes.

pages.title: Did you know?
pages.text: MFA is highly effective, but attackers may still look for ways around it by targeting people instead of the technology.

bullets.title: Push fatigue attacks are real
bullets.text: Attackers may spam approval requests and hope the user accepts one just to make them stop.

bullets.title: Text message MFA is better than nothing, but not the strongest option
bullets.text: SMS codes can be exposed through phone number attacks or SIM swapping.

bullets.title: Support impersonation is common
bullets.text: Attackers may pretend to be IT or help desk staff and ask users to share codes or approve a login.

bullets.title: MFA helps most when users stay alert
bullets.text: Even strong security controls work best when people verify requests and report suspicious activity quickly.

pages.title: Never share MFA codes
pages.text:
MFA codes are meant to verify your identity.

  • Never share MFA codes, approval prompts, or authentication requests with anyone.
  • This includes people claiming to be from IT, a bank, customer support, or another trusted organization.
  • Legitimate support staff should not ask for your MFA code for routine access.
  • If you receive an unexpected MFA prompt, do not approve it and report it using your organization's process.

MFA codes should stay private and be used only by the account owner.

pages.title: Real-world example: Uber MFA attack
pages.text:
In 2022, Uber reported a security incident in which an attacker used a contractor's stolen credentials and then targeted the contractor with repeated MFA approval requests.

Reporting on the incident also described the attacker using social engineering by pretending to be IT support, which helped convince the contractor to approve one of the MFA requests.

Once one request was accepted, the attacker gained access to internal systems.

This is a real-world example of how attackers may combine stolen passwords, push fatigue, and social engineering to get around MFA.

Even with MFA enabled, users should never approve an unexpected login request.

References: Uber Security Update | BleepingComputer coverage

image.alt: Example of repeated MFA approval requests

pages.title: What to do if something seems wrong
pages.text:
If you receive an unexpected MFA prompt or think your account may be under attack:

  1. Do not approve the request.
  2. Change your password if needed.
  3. Report the incident using your organization's process.
  4. Review your account activity if available.

Quick action helps reduce the risk of unauthorized access.

pages.subtitle: This short quiz will test your understanding of multi-factor authentication and safe MFA practices.
